articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: the Amistad and early Black Philadelphia writers
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Recounts the 1839 Amistad revolt led by Cinque, defended before the Supreme Court by John Quincy Adams and Senator Baldwin, and the Philadelphia meeting held to support the Africans by abolitionists including Rev. Dr. Wylie, Albert Barnes, George H. Stuart, Revs. Charles Gardner, Walter Proctor and Samuel Cornish, Jacob C. White, Robert Purvis, John P. Burr, Frederick Hinton and James McCrummell (at Arthur Tappan's instance). The second half surveys early Black Philadelphia writers: Absalom Jones, Russell Parrott, Bishop Richard Allen (d. 1832), Sarah M. Douglass, Sarah Forten, William Whipper, James and Robert Forten, Daniel A. Payne, Frances Ellen Watkins Harper, Frank Webb, Jarena Lee, Jacob C. White Jr., Parker T. Smith and Charlotte Forten Grimke.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: Absalom Jones
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: A biography of Absalom Jones, born enslaved in Delaware Nov. 6, 1746, brought to Philadelphia in 1762, self-educated, married in 1770 (buying his wife's freedom) and manumitted in 1784. Details his founding and 22-year rectorship of St. Thomas' Church, his role as treasurer of the 1796 insurance company, patronage of Russell Parrott (1814 address) and Prince Saunders, help raising Black soldiers in the War of 1812 and the first secular convention of 1817; also references Richard Allen, James Forten, the St. George's M.E. pulling-from-knees incident, and Benjamin Lundy's Genius of Universal Emancipation and Garrison.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: the American Negro Historical Society (and the 1838 convention)
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Opens with a rare printed report of a convention (Council of the colored people) held in Philadelphia June 4-9, 1838, then narrates the history of the American Negro Historical Society, conceived in fall 1897 by Robert M. Adger with organizers Alfred S. Cassey, Edward Jarris, Hans Shadd, Robert Jones, Theophilus J. Minton and Mrs. F.E.W. Harper. Lists early members and lecturers including Revs. Dr. Henry L. Phillips, William H. Dorsey, Matthew Anderson, P. Albert Dutreuille, Chris J. Perry, and lectures by W.E.B. DuBois, John W. Cromwell, Alice Moore Dunbar, Alain LeRoy Locke and others; Bolivar himself lectured on the Institute for Colored Youth.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: the Citizens Republican Club
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: A history of the Citizens Republican Club as it enters its 29th year, founded in 1884 by the late Andrew F. Stevens to counter corrupt ward politics and unite reputable Black men behind honest party work (first tested backing Henry K. Boyer for State Treasurer). Names founding subscribers such as John W. Page, Sylvester Coleman, William Warrick, Dr. E.C. Howard and Charles L. Ferrette, and lists successive presidents from Stevens through John C. Asbury to Amos Scott; largely a late-19th-century civic subject.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: Fanny Jackson Coppin and the Institute for Colored Youth
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: A tribute to Fanny M. Jackson (later Mrs. Levi J. Coppin), engaged at the Institute for Colored Youth in 1865 on Ebenezer D. Bassett's suggestion alongside Mary J. Patterson (the first two Black women to graduate from Oberlin). Recounts her rise to principal of the I.C.Y. in 1869, her oratory and pioneering industrial-education program later transferred to Cheyney, and her circle including Octavius V. Catto, William Still, Rev. Stephen Smith, Isaiah C. Wear, William Whipper and Richard T. Greener.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: the American Negro Historical Society
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: A near-parallel account of the American Negro Historical Society, founded fall 1897 by Robert M. Adger with Alfred S. Cassey, Edward Jarris, Hans Shadd, Robert Jones, Theophilus J. Minton and Mrs. F.E.W. Harper, plus early members Revs. Dr. Henry L. Phillips, William H. Dorsey and P. Albert Dutreuille. Notes the society's gift collections from Mrs. Amelia White (widow of Jacob C. White Jr.) and Adger, and catalogs lectures by W.E.B. DuBois, Daniel Murray, John W. Cromwell, Alice Moore Dunbar, Alain LeRoy Locke, Caroline R. LeCount and others.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: Black Philadelphia trades before the Civil War
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Surveys the trades and livelihoods Black Philadelphians created before the Civil War: porters who dominated Market Street hauling before 1835 (among the last, Robert Johnson, who died near 100 at the Home for Aged and Infirm Colored Persons), peripatetic janitors like Job Escoby and Joshua D. Kelly, printer-dentist James McCrummell, chimney sweeps (John Lewton, a blind man named Davis), and bootblacks at the Continental Hotel. Also describes hot-corn, pepper-pot and other San Domingo/Haitian-born women vendors around Independence Hall and the Kensington shipyards.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: James Forten and antislavery bookmaking
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Discusses antislavery bookmaking contemporary with James Forten, funded largely by Arthur Tappan, Arnold Buffum and William Whipper against a hostile press, and foreign travelers who profiled Forten, notably J.S. Abdy (1833) and Captain Marryat, whose 1839 book praised the intelligence and respectability of Philadelphia's free colored people. Marryat's description of an elaborate Black funeral is inferred to be that of Robert Bogle, officiated by Rev. William Douglass, the only minister then wearing vestments.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: James Forten's fight for the vote
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Continues quoting Captain Marryat's circa-1837 observations of Philadelphia's water supply and the contempt free Black people faced in America versus England. Centers on James Forten's court case appealing his denial of the vote as a free, taxpaying citizen worth $150,000 (the verdict went against him), presenting Forten as an indispensable early race figure alongside Crispus Attucks, Richard Allen, Absalom Jones, Phillis Wheatley and Benjamin Banneker.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: mutual-aid societies and the Free African Society
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Traces Black mutual-aid and beneficial societies beginning with the Free African Society (Philadelphia, 1787), which dissolved by 1791 into Episcopal and A.M.E. communions, and inspired similar bodies in Newport and Charleston's Brown Fellowship. Focuses on the Friendly Sons of St. Thomas (1798), whose officers included James Forten, John Bowers, Absalom Jones and William Coleman, and on Russell Parrott (d. Aug. 1824), quoting his tombstone; also names later societies honoring Richard Allen, Sarah Allen, Benjamin Lundy, Clayton Durham and Anthony Benezet.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: pioneer Black newspapers and magazines
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: A history of early Black newspapers and magazines, crediting John Russwurm of New York (Columbia-trained) as pioneer editor, and Philadelphia's Demosthenian Shield (1841), edited by Benjamin Stanley with contributors James McCrummell, John C. Bowers and William Whipper. Argues the Moral Reformer (1835), edited by William Whipper with supporters Dr. J.J.G. Bias, Dr. Belfast Burton and Jacob C. White, predated the A.M.E. Magazine (George Hogarth, Rev. Daniel A. Payne, David Ware) as the pioneer Black magazine.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: racial unity and Black historical study
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: An essay urging racial unity and historical study, citing the 1787 cohesion of Richard Allen, Absalom Jones, James Forten and William Gray and the 1817 defeat of colonization. Highlights Black historical organizations - the Yonkers Society of Negro Historical Research, the American Negro Academy founded by Rev. Dr. Alexander Crummell, and the American Negro Historical Society - and laments ignorance of figures like Frederick Douglass and the recently deceased Harriet Tubman.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: early white friends of the enslaved in Pennsylvania
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Chronicles early white friends of the enslaved in Pennsylvania: Francis Daniel Pastorius of Germantown (1688 protest), Benjamin Lay (c. 1750), the French Quaker convert Anthony Benezet (whose 1770 school trained many later Free African Society founders), Benjamin Franklin (first Abolition Society) and Benjamin Rush. Recounts Black leaders Allen, William Gray and Absalom Jones during the 1793 yellow fever, and later abolitionist allies including Bishop Alonzo Potter, Rev. Dr. Furness and David Paul Brown.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: fugitives, the Crafts, and the Christiana riot
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Contrasts modern hardships with the antebellum period, focusing on fugitive-slave episodes: the escape of William and Ellen Craft, aided at the Philadelphia Institute (715-17 Lombard St.) before being sent to Massachusetts and England, and the Christiana riot, in which Samuel Williams figured and was imprisoned. Also names Methodist ministers Clayton Durham, Walter Proctor and Joseph Cox who freed the enslaved via Maryland camp meetings, printer James McCrummell, and vigilance leaders Jacob C. White and Rev. Charles Gardner; notes Harden's, Lebanon and Olive cemeteries.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: Martin R. Delany in Philadelphia
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: A biography of Martin R. Delany, who spent several months of 1852 in Philadelphia revising and publishing (via King and Baird) his book on the condition, elevation, emigration and destiny of the colored people of the United States, while lecturing on phrenology and attending antislavery meetings. Notes his later commission as a major recruiting Black troops for Lincoln, and that his biographer Frances A. Rollin was an Institute for Colored Youth student who left just before graduating in 1863.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: Alfred H. Love and Philadelphia abolitionists
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: An obituary tribute to Philadelphia peace and antislavery reformer Alfred H. Love, who refused to ride segregated streetcars and supported the Underground Railroad, followed by a roll of fellow abolitionists: Lydia White (Free Labor store), Garrisonian merchant Charles Wise, shoemaker-orator Henry Bruner, Isaac T. and Edward Hopper, Mary Grew, James and Lucretia Mott, William and Deborah Wharton, the Shipley family, Passmore Williamson (imprisoned for aiding escapes) and Rev. Dr. William H. Furness.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: the Free African Society and the churches
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Argues for the primacy of the Free African Society (Philadelphia, 1787-1791) as forerunner of the Black churches, drawing on Douglass's Annals and new findings by Rev. Dr. Henry L. Phillips (from Christ Church records via Rev. Dr. Dorr) showing an S.P.G. catechist for Negroes, Mr. Sturgeon, ordained and serving Christ Church from 1747 to 1766. Notes that after the Society dissolved, members funded the St. Thomas lot at Fifth and Adelphi, the largest donor being Quaker-affiliated Cyrus Bustill.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: the Underground Railroad and the Vigilance Committee
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Reviews three Underground Railroad histories - William Still's (the fullest, first-hand and by a Black man), Dr. R.C. Smedley's (Chester County, drawing on Robert Purvis and Marianna Gibbons) and William H. Siebert's - set against Philadelphia's antislavery office at 105-107 N. Fifth St. Recounts the Longwood meetings of Garrison, Phillips, Lucretia Mott, Frederick Douglass and Sojourner Truth, Harriet Tubman's rescues, Box Brown's escape, and the Philadelphia Vigilance Committee roster including John P. Burr, Rev. Jeremiah Asher, N. Depee, William Still, I.C. Wear, Jacob C. White and Charles Wise.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: Black Philadelphia and the 1863 Gettysburg emergency
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Describes Philadelphia's Black response to the 1863 Gettysburg/Confederate invasion, when a company was raised at the Philadelphia Institute (715-17 Lombard St.) under Captain William H. Babe. Names Institute for Colored Youth teachers Jacob C. White Jr. and Octavius V. Catto and scholar-recruits (Lombard L. Nickens, Joseph G. Anderson Jr. and others); the ninety men were mustered in at Harrisburg but then refused, and later Philadelphians enlisted in the 54th and 55th Massachusetts and regiments raised at Camps Cadwalader and William Penn.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: Rev. Dr. John B. Reeve and Black Philadelphia in 1861
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: A tribute to the 82-year-old Rev. Dr. John B. Reeve, who came from New York during the Civil War to pastor Central Presbyterian Church (succeeding Revs. Stephen H. Gloucester, Ennels Adams and William Templeton), serving members like James Prosser, John McKee and William Still. Surveys the small Black Philadelphia of 1861 - its Presbyterian, Episcopal (St. Thomas, Crucifixion), Baptist and Methodist (Bethel, Wesley, Morris Brown) churches and few streetcar lines - and mentions arriving free Charleston mechanics.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: Black-owned Philadelphia businesses in 1861
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: A detailed directory of Black-owned Philadelphia businesses in 1861 - restaurateurs (James Prosser, Henry Minton, Maj. Johnson), caterers Thomas J. Dorsey and Henry Jones, barbers, James McCrummell's dental-supply works, William Still at the antislavery office, and Smith and Whipper's coal and lumber yard - anchored by Jacob C. White's rounds to the Philadelphia Institute and Lebanon Cemetery. Continues the John B. Reeve narrative, noting David B. Bowser (portrait painting), the Institute for Colored Youth at 816-18 Lombard, the Banneker Institute, and 16,000 Black men sent to war from Philadelphia.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: antebellum Black men of wealth
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Critiques the recent National Business Men's League convention and corrects a false claim that only now do Black men hold $50,000 fortunes, citing antebellum men of wealth such as the Westons and DeReef of Charleston, and Pennsylvania's Forten, Whipper, Cassey, Dorsey, McKee and Purvis. Emphasizes James Forten financing Garrison's Liberator in 1831, Cassey's gifts to Lundy, and Stephen Smith's $152,000 to the Home and his building of the Banneker House resort at Cape May around 1845.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: the Free African Society founders of 1787
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Argues that organized Black self-government pivots on 1787 Philadelphia and the Free African Society, spotlighting the largely self-taught Absalom Jones, elected its Overseer until dissolution in 1791. Lists the founders signing its preamble - Absalom Jones and Richard Allen first, then Samuel Boston, Joseph Johnson, Catto Freeman, Caesar Cranchall, James Potter, William White, Cyrus Bustill and others - and frames them, alongside Garrison's later 1831 work, as pioneers refuting charges of racial inferiority.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: Philadelphia firsts and the evangelist Julia Pell
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Catalogs Philadelphia firsts (Free African Society 1787, the 1817 and 1830 conventions, Jarena Lee's ordination by Bishop Allen in 1828), then tells the story of evangelist Julia Pell, daughter of a fugitive slave who took the name Pell after being freed through Isaac T. Hopper. Julia became a noted Methodist street and pulpit preacher in New York, her life chronicled in Lydia Maria Child's Letters from New York, and she shared the interest attaching to Rev. Jarena Lee.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: The Spectator and Alexander W. Davis
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Examines The Spectator, a small independent weekly founded in 1880 by Alexander W. Davis (an early Black police appointee under Mayor King), quoting its personal items on figures like I.C. Wear, Central Church trustees, and the young pastor Rev. L.J. Coppin of Bethel. Notes items on the Tassonian literary society, a Bethel Literary lecture by Rev. Solomon P. Hood, Fanny Jackson (Coppin) and her choristers at a Working Men's Club art exhibition, and praise of Jacob C. White Jr.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: the Bustill family
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Prompted by the Bustill family reunion, this recounts the clan from Samuel Bustill (a New Jersey provincial secretary noted by Benjamin Franklin) and his son Cyrus Bustill - Washington's Revolutionary-War baker, a founder of the 1787 Free African Society who donated to St. Thomas' Church despite his Quakerism, married to a woman of Delaware (Lenni-Lenape) descent. Traces descendants including son David Bustill (plasterer) and daughter Grace Bustill, wife of Robert Douglass and key to John Gloucester's founding of First African Presbyterian Church (1807); cites Rev. William T. Catto's church history.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: the 1833 American Anti-Slavery convention at McCrummell's house
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Recounts the 1833 founding of the American Anti-Slavery Society convention in Philadelphia at the house of James McCrummell, whose Declaration of Sentiments was signed by three Black men - James G. Barbadoes, James McCrummell and Robert Purvis - with Jacob White among secretaries and Sarah M. Douglass secretary of the women's association. Describes the 1883 fiftieth-anniversary celebration at Horticultural Hall, quoting Whittier's recollections, with surviving participants Robert Purvis (president), J. Miller McKim, James Mott, Arnold Buffum, William Still, Dr. Furness, Mary Grew and William D. Forten.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: Black Philadelphia burial grounds
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: A history of Black Philadelphia burial grounds: the first plot at St. Thomas' Church (Fifth and Adelphi), holding Rev. Absalom Jones, James Forten, Russell Parrott, Frank Johnson, Robert Bogle and John Bowers; Bethel's grounds (Bishop Allen in a crypt) and its lost Queen Street records; the Montier family plot (c. 1800); and John Harden's speculative cemetery (c. 1834). Notes the later founding of Olive and Lebanon Cemeteries by Jacob C. White, Nicholas G. Bolivar, David B. Bowser and Rev. Stephen Smith, shrouder Sarah Ash of the 1849 cholera, and the great funerals of Frank Johnson, James Forten and Octavius V. Catto (1871).

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: Hezekiah Grice and the 1830 Colored Convention
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Profiles Hezekiah Grice, the Baltimorean (documented in John W. Cromwell's account of the founder of the convention movement) who, amid Lundy and Garrison's early work and David Walker's Appeal, conceived the colored convention movement, securing Bishop Richard Allen's support for the 1830 meeting held at Bethel Church. Recounts Grice finding the aged Allen attending anatomy lectures, the convention's success despite Philadelphia press hostility, and delegates including James Deaver, Aaron Wilson, Robert Cowley, Belfast Burton, Shadrach Bassett and Bishop Morris Brown, plus its proposal for a manual training school.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points: racial passing in Philadelphia
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Reflecting on The Autobiography of an Ex-Colored Man and a long list of race-problem novels, Bolivar discusses racial passing and mixed-race families in Philadelphia, including several connected with Shiloh Baptist Church during Rev. Jeremiah Asher's pastorate (from 1847), some descended from a Richmond slave dealer. He cites numerous cases of light-complexioned Philadelphians who entered white society, the professions, the press, and Civil War service, arguing that such concealment shows someone is being fooled.
